htmlContent:
The trigger of being misunderstood or not believed

I remember the exact feeling. My chest tightened, my throat closed, and a hot wave of rage mixed with despair washed through me. I was trying to explain something important, something true about my experience, and the person across from me just shook their head. They didn't get it. They thought I was exaggerating, being dramatic, or outright lying. I wanted to scream. Instead, I shut down.

That moment wasn't just frustrating. It was a full-body trigger, one that sent me spinning into a state of activation that took hours, sometimes days, to recover from. And I know I'm not alone in this. Being misunderstood or not believed is one of the most potent triggers for anyone with a history of complex trauma. It's not just about feeling annoyed. It's about feeling erased.

Here's the thing. When you grow up in an environment where your reality is constantly denied, where you're told you didn't see what you saw or feel what you felt, your nervous system learns that being disbelieved is a threat to your survival. It becomes wired into your very biology. So when someone questions your truth today, even in a minor way, your body responds as if your life is on the line. Because in a way, for the child you were, it was.

I want to walk through this with you. Not from a place of clinical distance, but from the messy, real experience of living with this trigger. Let's talk about what's actually happening in your brain and body when you feel misunderstood, and why it hurts so damn much.

What's really happening in your nervous system

Let me introduce you to Stephen Porges. He's the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, and his work explains so much about why being misunderstood feels like an attack. Porges showed that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. It's a process he calls neuroception. It happens below conscious awareness, in the depths of your autonomic nervous system.

When you're in a conversation and you feel that familiar flash of being dismissed or not believed, your neuroception picks up on it instantly. Your body doesn't wait to think, Is this actually dangerous? It just reacts. Your vagus nerve, the main highway of your parasympathetic nervous system, starts shifting you into a defensive state. You might feel your heart race, your muscles tense, your breath become shallow. That's your sympathetic nervous system kicking in, preparing you for fight or flight.

Or maybe you go the other way. You feel a sudden numbness, a fog descends, your voice gets quiet, and you can't find the words. That's your dorsal vagal system taking over, the part that shuts you down when things feel hopeless. It's the same response that made you freeze as a child when there was no way out.

Think about it. Your nervous system doesn't distinguish between someone not believing you about a minor disagreement and someone denying your abuse. It just registers the pattern: truth being rejected, reality being questioned. And it sounds the alarm. Every single time.

This is why you can't just "let it go" or "not take it personally." Your body is literally fighting for its survival. The trigger of being misunderstood or not believed is a direct line back to the original wound of being unseen and unheard. And that wound runs deep.

The ghost of the child who was never believed

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory stored in your mind. It's a set of incomplete responses trapped in your nervous system. When you were a child and you tried to tell someone about the abuse, the neglect, the chaos, and they didn't believe you or they minimized it, your body had a response. You wanted to fight, to scream, to run. But you couldn't. So those impulses got stuck.

Now, every time you feel misunderstood, your body tries to complete that old, frozen response. That's why the reaction feels so disproportionate. You're not just reacting to the present moment. You're reacting to every single time you weren't believed across your entire life. It's a cumulative trigger.

I remember once telling a therapist about something that happened in my childhood. She looked at me with a kind face and said, "That must have been hard." But something in her tone felt off, like she didn't quite believe the severity of it. My body went into full revolt. I felt hot, shaky, and nauseous. I wanted to leave and never come back. And I almost did.

But here's what I've learned. That reaction wasn't about her. It was about the four-year-old me who told my mother I was scared and she said, "Don't be dramatic." It was about the seven-year-old me who told a teacher I was being hurt at home and she called my parents to "sort it out." It was about the teenager me who finally spoke up and was met with silence and shame.

Your trigger is not a flaw. It's a signal. It's your system telling you that something important was never resolved. And that's not your fault. You didn't cause this. You survived it.

Why the wellness industry gets this wrong

I need to be direct with you here. A lot of the self-help world will tell you that being triggered by being misunderstood is a sign that you need to "work on your self-worth" or "stop seeking validation from others." They'll tell you to just breathe through it and let it go. And that advice can be actively harmful.

Look, I'm all for breathing. I do it every day. But when your nervous system is in a full-blown trauma response, telling yourself to "just breathe" can feel like gaslighting yourself. It can reinforce the very message that got you here: that your feelings are wrong, that your reactions are too much, that you should just get over it.

The truth is more complex. Yes, eventually we want to build capacity to be with these feelings without being consumed by them. But that starts with validation, not dismissal. It starts with someone, even if that someone is you, saying: "Of course you feel this way. It makes perfect sense given what you've been through."

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) by Bessel van der Kolk. He lays out clearly why trauma responses are not character flaws but biological adaptations. Reading it helped me stop hating myself for my reactions and start understanding them.

Another resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He names the emotional flashback for what it is and gives practical tools for navigating it without shame.

You don't need to fix yourself. You need to understand yourself. And that starts with honoring the trigger, not pathologizing it.

What actually helps when you're in it

So what do you do when you're standing there, face to face with someone who just doesn't get it, and your whole body is screaming? I can't give you a perfect formula, but I can share what's helped me and what the research suggests.

First, recognize the flashback. Pete Walker calls these "emotional flashbacks" where you're not reliving a memory visually, but you're reliving the feeling of being small and powerless. Just naming it can create a sliver of space. You can say to yourself, "This is a flashback. I am not a child anymore. I am safe right now." Even if it feels false, say it anyway.

Second, orient to the present. Dan Siegel talks about the "window of tolerance," the zone where you can think and feel at the same time. When you're triggered, you're outside that window. To get back in, you need to ground yourself. Look around the room. Name three things you see. Feel your feet on the floor. Touch something solid. This isn't about bypassing the feeling. It's about telling your nervous system that the threat is not here anymore.

Third, and this is the hard one, give yourself permission to leave. You don't have to stay in a conversation where you're being invalidated. You can say, "I need to take a break," and walk away. Your nervous system needs to know it has an exit. If you force yourself to stay and endure, you reinforce the old pattern of being trapped.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). It's not a cure, but it can help support your nervous system's ability to regulate, especially after a triggering event. I take it at night and it helps me sleep more deeply.

And if you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn. It explores how family trauma can be passed down and how being misunderstood might have roots in generations before you.

Stay with me here. None of this is about making the trigger go away forever. It's about building a relationship with it. It's about learning to say, "I see you. I know you're here to protect me. Thank you. But I've got this now." And sometimes that works, and sometimes it doesn't. And that's okay too.

The painful gift of being misunderstood

I'm not going to wrap this up in a neat bow. Because there's nothing neat about it. Being misunderstood or not believed hurts. It always will, on some level. That's not a failure of healing. That's a sign that you care about being seen. That you have a truth that matters.

What changes over time is your capacity to hold that hurt without it destroying you. You learn to differentiate between the person who genuinely can't understand you and the person who refuses to. You learn to protect your truth without needing everyone to validate it. You learn that your experience is real whether or not anyone else confirms it.

That's not easy. Some days it feels impossible. But it's the work. And you're already doing it, just by reading this, just by being curious about why you react the way you do.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read those words. Maybe there's a softening, a recognition. Maybe there's resistance. Both are valid. Both are part of you.

You deserved to be believed as a child. You deserved to be seen and heard and taken seriously. And the fact that you weren't is not your fault. It never was.

Now, as an adult, you get to decide who has access to your truth. You get to choose who is safe enough to hear it. And you get to be the one who believes yourself, even when no one else does.

That's not a small thing. That's everything.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) by Bessel van der Kolk
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker
  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link)
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn
